hero.subtitle: Hash your files locally. Anchor the proof in Bitcoin, Ethereum, and multiple independent timestamping authorities. Download a court-ready certificate. Verifiable forever — even if this site disappears.
hero.riskReversal: If this site disappears tomorrow, your proof keeps working — Bitcoin nodes and RFC 3161 verifiers are completely independent of us.

drop.title: Drop your source files here
drop.subtitle: or click to browse — multiple files and folders supported

deposit.sectionTitle: Drop your source code — zero-knowledge architecture
deposit.sectionSub: SHA-256 hashing happens entirely in your browser via WebCrypto. Your files never leave your machine. The only thing that reaches our infrastructure is the 32-byte fingerprint.

gpg.title: Sign with GPG key
gpg.help: Paste your armored PGP private key to cryptographically sign the manifest. The key is used once and never stored.
gpg.pasteKey: Paste armored private key (-----BEGIN PGP PRIVATE KEY BLOCK-----)
gpg.skipped: Author identity is self-declared (no cryptographic signature).
gpg.skipHint: Skip this step if you don't have a GPG key — the WHAT and WHEN proofs still work.

anchors.title: Independent timestamp anchors
anchors.help: The manifest fingerprint is submitted to multiple independent sources in parallel. Each one is a separate piece of evidence: if any single source survives a dispute, the proof holds.

result.title: Certificate ready
result.btcNote: Bitcoin anchor pending — the manifest hash has been submitted to OpenTimestamps. After the next Bitcoin block (~10 min), the proof is immutable and independent of this site.

how.title: From files to legal-grade proof — in minutes
how.subtitle: Watch the full pipeline: file detection, SHA-256 hashing, GPG signing, manifest building, parallel anchoring in Bitcoin + RFC 3161 + Ethereum, and certificate issuance — all happening end-to-end, live.
how.step1Desc: SHA-256 of every file is computed in your browser via WebCrypto. Files never leave your machine.
how.step2Desc: Hashes are bundled into a CITATION.cff manifest. Optionally sign it with your GPG key for cryptographic authorship proof.
how.step3Desc: The manifest hash is submitted to Bitcoin via OpenTimestamps, to RFC 3161 timestamping authorities, and to Ethereum L2. Nobody — not even you — can change the date afterwards.
how.step4Desc: You get a printable PDF certificate plus the raw proof files. Anyone can independently verify every layer — without trusting us.

pipeline.detectDesc: The browser reads file headers and metadata — type, size, author, last-modified — without uploading anything. Local inspection only.
pipeline.hashDesc: SHA-256 fingerprint of every byte, computed in-browser via WebCrypto. 32 bytes. Change one pixel, change the hash.
pipeline.signDesc: Optional: detached PGP signature with your GPG key. Binds the manifest to your public identity so courts can verify authorship with just `gpg --verify`.
pipeline.manifestDesc: All hashes + author metadata bundle into a CITATION.cff document — a portable YAML standard used in academic citation, fully human-readable.
pipeline.anchorDesc: Fingerprint submitted in parallel to Bitcoin (OpenTimestamps) + 3 RFC 3161 TSAs + Ethereum L2. Five independent proofs of the exact moment of creation.
pipeline.certificateDesc: Printable PDF with every proof, fingerprint, and verification instructions. Sharable by email, uploadable to court filings, valid forever — even if this site disappears.

why.title: Why Bitcoin?
why.p1: GPG signatures prove WHO signed something — but the timestamp inside a signature is just whatever the signer's clock said. It can be backdated trivially. The time problem must be solved separately.
why.p2: Bitcoin fixes this. Once a hash lands inside a block, claiming it existed earlier is mathematically impossible — the entire proof-of-work chain would have to be rewritten by the global mining network.
why.p3: OpenTimestamps aggregates millions of hashes into a single Merkle root and writes it on-chain. Your proof is a tiny binary file that can be verified offline against any Bitcoin node. No trust in mayday.software required.
why.p4: This is no longer theoretical. On 20 March 2025 the Paris Judicial Tribunal accepted a Bitcoin/OpenTimestamps anchor as proof of creation date — the first ruling of its kind in continental Europe.
why.alt: Bitcoin is not the only anchor. RFC 3161 Timestamping Authorities (including eIDAS-qualified TSAs) provide instant, legally binding timestamps in the EU. Ethereum L2 adds a third independent chain. Multiple independent stamps make the proof effectively unforgeable.

legal.title: Accepted as legal evidence
legal.p1: Under the Berne Convention (Art. 5), copyright arises automatically upon creation — registration is nowhere required. What matters in court is proof of authorship and the date a particular version existed.
legal.p2: In the EU, Bitcoin-anchored timestamps qualify as advanced electronic timestamps under eIDAS 910/2014. The Paris Tribunal accepted them as evidence (March 2025). RFC 3161 timestamps from eIDAS-qualified TSAs go further — they carry a legal presumption of accuracy under Art. 41.
legal.p3: Important — this is a complement, not a replacement. In Russia the Supreme Court (ruling № 305-ЭС17-4311, 2020) confirmed that deposit alone does not create a presumption of authorship. Only state registration at Rospatent under Art. 1262 of the Civil Code does. mayday.software doesn't replace it — it adds an independent cryptographic layer that costs nothing, takes minutes, and survives any single institution.
legal.p4: Use it like signed git commits: one of several independent pieces of evidence. Combined with Rospatent, n'RIS, or a notary, a cryptographic hash anchored in a public chain is extremely difficult for any expert to discredit.

verify.title: Verify a deposit
verify.subtitle: Drop your manifest, proof files, and optionally the source files to verify the deposit independently.
verify.dropSources: Drop source files (optional — for hash re-verification)
verify.dropPublicKey: Paste or drop public GPG key (optional)
verify.readinessHint: Manifest is required. Other files enable deeper checks.
verify.keyPassphraseHint: Looks like you uploaded a passphrase-locked private key. We use only the public material to verify the signature — the passphrase is consumed locally and never stored.
verify.keyPassphraseRequired: The uploaded key is private and encrypted — please enter its passphrase above and run verification again.
verify.otsParsed: OpenTimestamps proof parsed — digest matches manifest
verify.otsDigestMismatch: OTS digest does not match manifest
verify.otsTooShort: File too short to be a valid .ots
verify.cliHint: For full chain verification, use CLI: ots verify / openssl ts -verify

trust.title: Built for legal-grade evidence
trust.subtitle: Your proof chain rests on international legal frameworks and open cryptographic standards — not on our continued existence.
trust.wipoDesc: Follows WIPO guidelines for digital-asset proof. Berne Convention Art. 5 — copyright is automatic, but evidence wins cases.
trust.parisDesc: Paris Judicial Tribunal (Mar 2025) accepted a Bitcoin/OpenTimestamps anchor as proof of creation date — first ruling of its kind in continental Europe.
trust.eidasDesc: RFC 3161 timestamps from EU-qualified TSAs carry legal presumption of accuracy under eIDAS Art. 41 (Regulation 910/2014).
trust.privacyDesc: Files never leave your browser. Only a 32-byte SHA-256 fingerprint reaches our infrastructure. No accounts. No tracking. No vendor lock-in.

useCases.title: Built for anyone who creates
useCases.subtitle: Wherever proof of authorship matters — code, design, writing, legal artifacts — a deposit gives you an independent timestamp anyone can verify.
useCases.devDesc: Lock the exact source state of a release before filing a patent or shipping to clients. Plays nicely with git tags and CI.
useCases.designerDesc: Stamp a Figma kit before pitching. If a client recycles your work, you have a Bitcoin-anchored proof of who showed it first.
useCases.writerDesc: Anchor manuscript drafts and dataset versions as you write. Survives any single platform — works for theses, novels, scientific papers.
useCases.legalDesc: Batch-deposit client artifacts via REST API, attach the certificate PDF and raw proofs to case files. Independent, court-ready evidence.

compare.title: Where deposit fits among existing options
compare.subtitle: We don't replace notaries or state registries — we add a free, independent layer that survives any single institution. Here's how the major options compare.
compare.footnote: Sources: Paris Judicial Tribunal (March 2025) · eIDAS 910/2014 Art. 41 · RU Supreme Court ruling № 305-ЭС17-4311 (2020).

pricing.title: Always free for the core deposit
pricing.subtitle: No account, no upsell screens, no card on file. The same cryptographic guarantees as paid registries — minus the bureaucracy.
pricing.point1Desc: always free for individuals and teams alike — no rate limit, no watermark, no trial period
pricing.point2Desc: the worker is ~400 lines of TypeScript; fork on GitHub, deploy to your own Cloudflare account in 5 minutes
pricing.point3Desc: for IP firms, registries and SaaS platforms — early-access via the GitHub waitlist

cta.finalSub: Drop your files, generate a court-ready certificate, and download six independent proofs — all in under two minutes, without an account.

integrations.title: Automate your IP protection
integrations.subtitle: REST API, webhooks, CMS embeds — build copyright deposits into any CI/CD pipeline, Zapier workflow, or website.
integrations.apiDesc: Submit digests, retrieve proofs, and download certificates programmatically. API-key auth, JSON responses, instant results.
integrations.zapierDesc: Trigger a deposit from any CI/CD pipeline, GitHub Action, or Zapier workflow. Receive proof URLs via webhook callback.
integrations.cmsDesc: One script tag in WordPress, Shopify, or any site. Your visitors see a "Protected by mayday.software" trust badge with verification link.
integrations.whitelabelDesc: Run your own branded deposit service. Full API access, custom domain, your branding — built for IP law firms, registries, and SaaS platforms.

footer.tagline: Open source · Files stay in your browser · Forever verifiable
footer.disclaimer: All hashing and signing runs locally in your browser. We do not store or transmit your files. Open-source.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.a1: A free, open-source tool that creates cryptographic proof of authorship and creation date. It hashes your files in the browser, anchors the fingerprint in Bitcoin, Ethereum, and RFC 3161 timestamping authorities, and gives you a court-ready certificate.
faq.a2: Yes. Under the Berne Convention, copyright arises automatically — what matters in court is evidence. Bitcoin timestamps qualify as advanced electronic timestamps under eIDAS 910/2014, and the Paris Tribunal accepted them as evidence in March 2025. RFC 3161 timestamps from eIDAS-qualified TSAs carry a legal presumption of accuracy (Art. 41).
faq.q3: Do my files leave my computer?
faq.a3: No. SHA-256 hashing runs entirely in your browser via WebCrypto. Only the 32-byte fingerprint reaches our infrastructure. We never see, store, or transmit your files.
faq.a4: Your proof still works. Bitcoin anchors are verified against any Bitcoin node. RFC 3161 timestamps are verified with OpenSSL against the TSA's public certificate. The proof files are self-contained and independent of this site.
faq.q5: How is this different from a notary?
faq.a5: A notary certifies a single snapshot in person. mayday.software lets you create unlimited timestamps instantly, for free, with mathematical proof that no one — not even you — can backdate. It complements (not replaces) notarial acts.
faq.q6: What file types can I protect?
faq.a6: Anything. Source code, designs, documents, images, music, video, datasets — if it's a file, it can be hashed. There are no format or size restrictions.
faq.a7: Yes. No accounts, no limits, no hidden fees. The core deposit service is and will remain free. Future premium features (API, white-label, monitoring) will fund the project.
faq.q8: Can I use this as evidence in court?
faq.a8: The deposit produces multiple independent pieces of evidence: Bitcoin blockchain anchors, RFC 3161 timestamps from certified authorities, and optionally GPG signatures. Combined they form a strong evidentiary chain. Use it alongside other evidence — registered IP, signed commits, notarial acts — for the strongest case.
